original_hypothesis.title: Meaningless Address Validation Allows Bricking Gauge Fee Distribution
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The setInternalBribe function in GaugeCL attempts to validate the new internal bribe address using `require(_int >= address(0))`. Because addresses are treated as unsigned integers in Solidity, this condition is always true, even for the zero address. If an admin accidentally sets the internal bribe to address(0), subsequent calls to _claimFees will attempt to approve and notify rewards on the zero address, causing the transaction to revert and permanently blocking fee distribution for that gauge.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Flawed input validation on critical configuration setters can lead to accidental protocol bricking. While it requires an admin mistake, the lack of safeguards makes it a notable operational risk.

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Admin error sets critical contract pointer to zero
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No profit, causes DoS on fee distribution

economic_questions: Can the internal bribe be reset to a valid address after being set to zero?
validation_requirements: Test setting internal bribe to address(0)
Verify _claimFees reverts when internal_bribe is address(0)

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Locks LP fees in the gauge

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: veNFT voters expecting LP fees from the affected gauge
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Trading fees for the specific pool

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm revert on zero address interaction

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
